planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens after the last frost in spring into moist, fertile, well-drained soil. In zone 7, mulch the root zone heavily in late autumn to protect the roots from hard freezes.

directions: Plant in partial sun with rich, evenly moist soil; in hotter climates, afternoon shade helps prevent wilting and keeps the large leaves looking their best through summer. The enormous domed clusters of fragrant rose-pink flowers appear from July through September and are highly attractive to butterflies. Be aware that this shrub spreads aggressively by root suckers, especially in zone 9 and warmer — install a root barrier or remove suckers promptly to prevent unwanted spread. In zones 7 and 8 the top growth is often killed by frost but roots reliably resprout in spring; cut dead stems to the ground and apply a thick mulch layer over the root zone each winter.

planting.note: Set out container-grown plants in early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil. Softwood cuttings taken in late spring root readily in a humid propagation frame and can be potted on and overwintered for planting the following year.

directions: Plant in full to partial sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Hybrid Deutzia tolerates a wide range of soil types as long as drainage is adequate. In late spring the gracefully arching branches are smothered in clusters of white to pale pink flowers that make this compact 3–5 ft shrub one of the most floriferous in the garden. Prune immediately after flowering by cutting one-third of the oldest stems to the ground each year; this keeps the plant vigorous and prevents the centre from becoming congested. Foliage is notably clean and disease-resistant, making it an easy-care alternative to more demanding flowering shrubs.

common_name: Scentless Mock Orange / Wild Mock Orange

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown stock in early spring in well-drained to dry soil; this species is native to rocky woodland slopes and bluffs and tolerates lean, shallow soils well. Softwood cuttings taken in early summer root readily and are the most practical propagation method.

directions: Plant in partial sun with good air circulation in well-drained to dry soil; unlike the common Mock Orange, this native species thrives in lean, rocky soils along woodland edges and bluffs without supplemental fertilization. The large, pure white four-petaled flowers appear in late spring and, while scentless, are showy enough to be a garden highlight and attract native pollinators. Allow ample space — established shrubs reach 6–10 ft in both height and spread and develop a bold, multi-stemmed arching form. Prune selectively after flowering, removing the oldest stems at the base every few years to rejuvenate the shrub and encourage strong new flowering wood.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in deep, moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil. Softwood cuttings taken in summer root under mist propagation; seeds should be sown fresh in autumn in a cold frame as they lose viability quickly.

directions: Plant in partial sun in deep, fertile, well-drained soil with protection from harsh wind; Dipelta floribunda develops into a large arching shrub of 10–15 ft and needs generous space to display its full elegance. In late spring, fragrant pale pink flowers with distinctive orange-yellow throats cover the gracefully drooping branches in a display reminiscent of a giant Weigela. After the flowers fade, the papery, wing-like seed bracts persist through summer and add ornamental interest that extends the plant's season of appeal. Prune lightly after flowering to maintain shape, removing only the oldest and weakest stems; heavy pruning is unnecessary and detracts from the natural arching habit.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or balled-and-burlapped stock in early autumn to allow root establishment before winter. Seeds require 60–90 days of cold stratification and are best sown outdoors in autumn for natural stratification and spring germination.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, deep, well-drained to seasonally wet soil; Green Ash is one of the most adaptable native trees in eastern North America and tolerates periodic flooding, clay soils, and urban conditions better than most hardwoods. It is the fastest-growing native ash species and provides dense shade and important wildlife value — the winged seeds are consumed by many bird species including finches and grosbeaks. All plantings of this species should be made with full awareness of the emerald ash borer threat: consult your local extension service for current biocontrol and treatment options and consider whether site conditions justify planting. Established trees need little care beyond occasional formative pruning of crossing branches in late winter; avoid wounding the bark unnecessarily, as entry points can invite opportunistic pests.
